stem: A decision-maker must choose among 4 investment portfolio options. The probability of favorable market conditions is uncertain, estimated between 32% and 46%. The payoffs (in $K) for each option are: bonds: $298K in good market conditions, $96K in bad market conditions. index fund: $192K in good market conditions, $36K in bad market conditions. tech stocks: $275K in good market conditions, $84K in bad market conditions. real estate: $248K in good market conditions, $120K in bad market conditions. Which option maximizes expected value? Does the optimal choice change across the range of probability estimates? What is the expected value of the best option?

ground_truth.answer: At P(good)=0.39: best option is bonds with EV=$174.8K. The optimal choice switches: real estate at P(good)=0.32, bonds at P(good)=0.46. EV range of best option: [161.0, 188.9].

solution_steps: Compute EV for each option: EV = P(good)×payoff_good + P(bad)×payoff_bad.
The optimal choice switches: real estate at P(good)=0.32, bonds at P(good)=0.46.
common_failure_modes: Evaluating options only at the midpoint probability without sensitivity analysis
Not identifying the breakeven probability where the optimal choice switches
Ignoring downside risk and focusing only on expected value
Not performing minimax regret analysis to find the most robust choice
